figures.gloss: The indivisible mystical unity of God (Hak), the Prophet (Muhammed), and the Saint (Ali). They are not separate entities but a singular continuous emanation of divine truth, representing the ultimate source, the exoteric messenger, and the esoteric gate.
figures.facet: Unlike orthodox trinities, this is an emanation of esoteric knowledge ('Batin'). Ali is elevated beyond a historical caliph into the eternal, cosmic manifestation of divine secrecy and justice.
figures.provenance: Core Alevi nefes (hymns); the Buyruk; poetry of Pir Sultan Abdal.

figures.gloss: The 13th-century Anatolian mystic who formalized the Alevi-Bektashi path. He famously held a lion and a gazelle in one arm to symbolize universal peace, fusing Central Asian nomadic spirituality with esoteric Islamic mysticism.
figures.facet: He is not merely a historical teacher but the 'Sercesme' (the fountainhead). His living spiritual authority anchors Anatolian humanism directly to cosmic truth, continually flowing through his successors.

figures.gloss: The foundational ritual gathering that replaces the orthodox mosque. Men and women sit together in a circle as equal souls ('Can'). Accompanied by the sacred lute, the community resolves grievances, shares a communal meal, and manifests the divine presence.
figures.facet: The Cem is a terrestrial recreation of the Assembly of the Forty (Kirklar Cemi) from the Prophet's Night of Ascension. God is found not in a building, but face-to-face in the human assembly.
figures.provenance: The Buyruk; oral transmission of the Dedes.

figures.gloss: The ecstatic, turning ritual dance performed during the Cem. Participants mirror the movements of planets orbiting the sun and the flight of the crane, accelerating to the rhythm of the baglama to shed the ego and achieve spiritual flight.
figures.facet: Far more than a folk dance, Semah is a mandatory act of worship where the human body itself becomes the moving axis mundi, completely rejecting the static postures of orthodox prayer.
figures.provenance: Ritual practice tracing to the Kirklar Cemi; Alevi nefes.

figures.gloss: The developmental architecture of the soul. The seeker progresses from Sharia (law), to Tariqat (the esoteric path), to Marifat (gnosis), and finally achieves Haqiqat (ultimate truth).
figures.facet: It systematically demotes orthodox religious law to a mere kindergarten of the soul. True religion only begins once the literal law is transcended for profound esoteric realization.

figures.gloss: A sacred, unbreakable bond of spiritual brotherhood sworn between two married couples in the presence of the Dede. They become 'yol kardesi' (siblings of the path), sharing all worldly debts, sins, and spiritual merits.
figures.facet: It overrides biological family. A soul cannot even enter the deepest levels of the Cem or approach the Divine without being yoked to another through this radical, communal accountability.
figures.provenance: The Buyruk; modeled on Muhammad and Ali's brotherhood at Medina.

figures.gloss: The ritual posture of absolute surrender performed in the Cem. The seeker stands barefoot, right big toe over the left, arms crossed, offering their life and ego to the community's judgment, echoing the martyr Mansur al-Hallaj.
figures.facet: It transforms the historical execution of a mystic into an everyday ritual requirement. Before entering the sacred assembly, one must willingly 'die before dying.'
figures.provenance: Alevi ritual choreography; Sufi martyrology of al-Hallaj.

figures.gloss: The hereditary spiritual leaders of the Alevi community, tracing their bloodline ('Evlad-i Resul') back to the Prophet Muhammad through the Twelve Imams. They lead the Cem, arbitrate disputes, and transmit esoteric knowledge.
figures.facet: They function almost entirely independently of exoteric Islamic structures. They are living vessels of divine authority whose legitimacy flows from blood and mystical initiation, bridging the roles of Islamic saint and Central Asian shaman.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The divine triad is invoked and physically manifested in the center of the sacred assembly.
Intra-tradition: The ecstatic rotation of the Semah serves as the kinetic climax and heart of the Cem.
Intra-tradition: Entering the profound spiritual communion of the Cem requires submitting to the ego-death of the Dar.
Intra-tradition: The Pir formalized the Four Doors and Forty Stations as the definitive architecture of the Alevi developmental path.
Intra-tradition: A soul cannot achieve full integration into the innermost rites of the Cem without being bound to a spiritual sibling.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Alevi Dede's hereditary authority represents a documented syncretism, layering Islamic Alid lineage over the Turkic concept of sacred, charismatic bloodlines.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Semah's ecstatic flight represents a historical retention of Central Asian shamanic trance practices, syncretized into Sufi mystical rotation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The physical and spiritual circle of the community, unbroken by hierarchy, where the divine presence is made immanent among the people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The profound theological realization that the human core is an emanation of divine truth, famously declared by the martyr al-Hallaj.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The esoteric principle that the macrocosmic divine truth must be systematically mapped and realized through the microcosmic journey of the human soul.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A foundational sage who shapes the spiritual and cultural landscape of an entire people through sacred song and esoteric wisdom.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A threefold manifestation of ultimate reality, expressing the absolute, its energetic transmission, and its physical realization on earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme reality that remains entirely ungraspable except through its emanated manifestations and the esoteric gates.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The profound elevation of a dyad; a spiritual pairing necessary to navigate the severe trials of the cosmos and approach the sacred.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An ecstatic, communal, rhythm-driven dance designed to collapse the boundary between the material and spiritual planes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly structured ritual assembly that collapses time, integrating the community while directly recreating primordial divine events.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Total vulnerability and the refusal of harm as the absolute prerequisite for spiritual purification, enacted physically before the assembly.

dossier.essence: A heterodox tradition of Islamic lineage in Anatolia, held today by millions of Turkish and Kurdish Alevis in Turkey and its diaspora, with Qizilbash and Bektashi historical roots. It centers on the unity Hak-Muhammed-Ali and the Twelve Imams, and on the claim that the inner meaning (batin) of religion outranks the outward law — God is sought face-to-face in the human being and the assembly, not in the mosque.
dossier.cosmos: Reality emanates from Hak (God/Truth), and the human being is its fullest mirror; the path aims at the perfected human (insan-i kamil) through the Four Doors — law, path, gnosis, truth. The primordial Assembly of the Forty (Kirklar Cemi), where Muhammad joined the gathered saints as an equal, is the charter of the ritual world. Devriye poetry frames existence as a circle — the soul descends from God through creation and climbs back — while concrete afterlife imagery is held lightly and varies by community.
dossier.practice: The congregational rite is the cem, led by a dede of hereditary sacred lineage: men and women together, hymns (nefes/deyis) to the bağlama lute, the turning semah dance, a shared meal, and the public airing and reconciliation of disputes — those at odds may not join the rite. Musahiplik binds two couples in lifelong mutual responsibility; the twelve-day Muharram fast mourns Karbala; pilgrimage centers on the shrine of Hacı Bektaş Veli. Alevis historically faced Ottoman persecution and still contest official recognition in Turkey.
dossier.canon: The Buyruk compilations, the Makalat attributed to Hacı Bektaş Veli, the Velayetname, and a vast sung corpus — above all the nefes of poets such as Pir Sultan Abdal and Shah Ismail (Hatayi); transmission is substantially oral.
dossier.reflects: The location of the sacred in the gathered community — worship as assembly, music, and reconciliation, with esoteric inwardness set deliberately against legalist orthodoxy.
sources: John Kingsley Birge, 'The Bektashi Order of Dervishes' (1937)
